by
  have h1 : HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P := hX
  have h2 : iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := by
    have h3 : iIndepFun (fun i ↦ fun ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P :=
      iIndepFun.comp_right h1.iIndepFun (fun i j ⟨h⟩ ⟨h'⟩ => by
        simp_all [Set.ext_iff]
        <;>
        (try tauto)
        <;>
        (try {
          have h₄ := h
          have h₅ := h'
          simp_all [Set.ext_iff]
          <;>
          (try tauto)
          <;>
          (try {
            have h₆ := h₄
            have h₇ := h₅
            simp_all [Set.ext_iff]
            <;>
            tauto
          })
        })
      )
    simpa using h3
  exact h2

by
  have h₁ : iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := by
    -- Use the fact that the measure is a product measure to deduce independence
    have h₂ : ∀ s : Finset ι, ∀ t : Finset ι, Disjoint s t →
        Indep (Pi (fun i ∈ s ↦ (fun ω ↦ i ∈ X ω))) (Pi (fun i ∈ t ↦ (fun ω ↦ i ∈ X ω))) P := by
      intro s t hdisj
      -- For disjoint finite sets of indices, the functions are independent because of the product structure
      have h₃ := hX
      simp [IsBernoulliOn] at h₃ ⊢
      -- Use properties of the product measure to establish independence
      have h₄ := h₃
      simp only [hasLaw] at h₄ ⊢
      -- Extract the product measure structure
      simp_all [MeasureTheory.IIndepFun]
      <;>
      (try simp_all [MeasureTheory.Indep]) <;>
      (try norm_num) <;>
      (try aesop) <;>
      (try
        {
          simp_all [MeasureTheory.IIndepFun]
          <;>
          aesop
        })
    -- Use the above result to show infinite independence
    have h₃ : iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := by
      refine' iIndepFun_of_disjoint_finite _
      intro s t hdisj
      have h₄ := h₂ s t hdisj
      simpa [Pi] using h₄
    exact h₃
  exact h₁

by
  have h₁ : iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := by
    have h₂ : HasLaw X (Ber(u, p)) P := hX
    have h₃ : iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := by
      have h₄ : HasLaw X (Ber(u, p)) P := h₂
      have h₅ : iIndepFun (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) P := by
        -- Use the fact that BernoulliOn is a product measure to deduce independence.
        have h₆ : ∀ (F : Finset ι), iIndepOn (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) F ⊥ P := by
          intro F
          have h₇ : iIndepOn (fun i ω ↦ i ∈ X ω) F ⊥ P := by
            -- Use the product structure of the measure to deduce independence over any finite subset F.
            sorry
          exact h₇
        -- Deduce the general independence from the finite case.
        exact iIndepFun_iff.mpr fun F ↦ h₆ F
      exact h₅
    exact h₃
  exact h₁

  -- Use the fact that the functions are independent to prove that the random variables are independent
